questions.question:
The root directory of a disk should be placed
A. at a fixed address in main memory
B. at a fixed location on the disk
C. anywhere on the disk
D. at a fixed location on the system disk
questions.answer: B. at a fixed location on the disk
questions.to_find: Where should the root directory of a disk be placed?

understand.plain: The root directory is the top-level directory of a file system. The OS must always be able to find it to navigate the entire directory tree. If its location were variable, the OS would have no starting point to locate files. Placing it at a fixed, known location on the disk ensures reliable access on every boot.

keywords.explain: The top-most directory in a file system hierarchy from which all other files and directories are reachable.
keywords.example: In Unix, '/' is the root; in Windows, 'C:\' is the root of drive C.

keywords.explain: A well-known, pre-determined sector/block on the disk where the OS always looks for the root directory metadata.
keywords.example: The boot sector (sector 0) or a reserved superblock area on a hard disk.

understand.visual_alt: A disk with a highlighted fixed sector labeled 'Root Directory', arrows from the OS boot process pointing to that sector to indicate predictable access.

given.aim: Determine the correct placement policy for the root directory of a disk.

terms.meaning: Top-level directory of the file system tree.
terms.connects: Must be locatable at boot time without any prior directory traversal.

terms.meaning: Volatile RAM; content lost on power off.
terms.connects: Cannot persist the root directory across power cycles.

terms.meaning: A specific, constant sector or block address on the storage medium.
terms.example: MBR at LBA 0, superblock at block 1 in ext2
terms.connects: Allows the OS to locate the root directory deterministically at boot without needing to search.

given.plan: Analyze each option: main memory is volatile (eliminated), 'anywhere on disk' requires a search mechanism (impractical at boot), 'fixed location on system disk' is too restrictive for non-boot disks. Only 'fixed location on the disk' ensures the OS can always locate the root directory for any mounted disk.

steps.title: Eliminate volatile/unreliable options
steps.apply: Option A (main memory) is volatile — the root directory would be lost on every power-off. Option C (anywhere on disk) would require the OS to scan the entire disk to find the root, which is infeasible during early boot. Option D (fixed location on the system disk) is too narrow — every disk that is mounted must have its own accessible root directory, not just the OS boot disk.
steps.note: The OS uses a bootstrap process that relies on well-known addresses; it cannot do a full scan early in boot.

steps.title: Confirm the correct answer
steps.apply: Option B says the root directory should be at a fixed location on the disk. This is exactly what every file system does: ext2/ext3 places the root inode at inode #2 at a known offset, FAT places the root directory at a fixed cluster, NTFS places $MFT at a fixed location. The OS reads this known address to begin any file lookup.
steps.note: This is a fundamental principle: the root of any mounted file system must be findable via a fixed, agreed-upon location on that very disk.

solution.result: The root directory of a disk should be placed at a fixed location on the disk (Option B).

questions.formula_note: File system bootstrap principle: the OS must know a fixed disk address for the root directory to begin directory traversal at mount/boot time.

questions.question:
If the overhead for formatting a disk is 96 bytes for a 512-byte sector,
A. Compute the unformatted capacity of the disk for the following parameters:
   - Number of surfaces: 8
   - Outer diameter of the disk: 12 cm
   - Inner diameter of the disk: 4 cm
   - Inner track space: 0.1 mm
   - Number of sectors per track: 20
B. If the disk in (A) is rotating at 360 rpm, determine the effective data transfer rate which is defined as the number of bytes transferred per second between disk and memory.
questions.answer: Part A: Unformatted capacity = 38,400,000 bytes (approx. 38.4 MB). Part B: Effective data transfer rate = 61,440 bytes/second.
questions.to_find: Unformatted disk capacity and effective data transfer rate.

understand.plain: A disk has multiple surfaces, each with many concentric tracks. Each track is divided into sectors. We first compute how many tracks fit on each surface given the physical dimensions, then multiply by sectors and bytes to get total capacity. The formatted overhead reduces usable space per sector. The transfer rate depends on how fast sectors pass the read head.

keywords.meaning: Total raw bytes on the disk before any formatting overhead is applied.
keywords.example: 8 surfaces x 40 tracks x 20 sectors x 512 bytes = raw capacity

keywords.meaning: The radial distance between adjacent tracks on a disk surface.
keywords.example: 0.1 mm track spacing means 10 tracks per mm of radial distance.

keywords.meaning: Net bytes of user data transferred per second, after deducting formatting overhead.
keywords.example: If a sector holds 512 raw bytes but 96 are overhead, only 416 bytes are user data per sector.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='340' height='200' viewBox='0 0 340 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='170' cy='100' r='90' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='170' cy='100' r='80' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><circle cx='170' cy='100' r='70' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><circle cx='170' cy='100' r='60' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><circle cx='170' cy='100' r='50' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><circle cx='170' cy='100' r='20' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='100' x2='170' y2='100' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='4'/><text x='110' y='92' class='sub ink'>Radius</text><text x='172' y='155' class='sub ink'>Tracks</text><text x='80' y='175' class='tag ink'>Outer r=6cm, Inner r=2cm</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Concentric circles representing disk tracks. Outer radius 6 cm, inner radius 2 cm, track spacing 0.1 mm. Each ring is a track; all tracks on a surface have the same number of sectors.

given.aim: Calculate unformatted disk capacity and effective data transfer rate.

terms.meaning: The disk pack has 8 read/write surfaces.
terms.connects: Total capacity multiplies by number of surfaces.
Determines how many tracks fit in the recordable radial band.

terms.term: Sectors per track = 20, bytes per sector = 512
terms.meaning: Each track is divided into 20 sectors of 512 bytes each.
terms.example: 20 sectors x 512 bytes = 10,240 bytes per track
terms.connects: Used to compute track capacity and total disk capacity.

terms.meaning: Disk spins at 360 revolutions per minute; 96 bytes per sector are used for formatting (sync, address, ECC marks).
terms.example: 512 - 96 = 416 bytes of user data per sector
terms.connects: RPM determines how many sectors pass the head per second; overhead reduces effective transfer rate.

given.plan: Step 1: Compute usable radial band = (outer radius - inner radius). Convert to mm and divide by track spacing to get tracks per surface. Step 2: Multiply tracks x surfaces x sectors/track x bytes/sector for unformatted capacity. Step 3: Compute rotational speed in rev/sec, multiply by sectors/rev to get sectors/sec, then multiply by net bytes/sector for effective transfer rate.

steps.title: Compute number of tracks per surface
steps.apply:
Outer radius = 12/2 = 6 cm = 60 mm. Inner radius = 4/2 = 2 cm = 20 mm.
Recordable band = 60 - 20 = 40 mm.
Tracks per surface = 40 mm / 0.1 mm = 400 tracks per surface.
steps.note: We use radius (half the diameter) for the radial distance calculation.

steps.title: Compute unformatted disk capacity
steps.formula_raw: Capacity = surfaces x tracks_per_surface x sectors_per_track x bytes_per_sector
steps.apply:
Capacity = 8 x 400 x 20 x 512
= 8 x 400 = 3200 tracks total
3200 x 20 = 64,000 sectors total
64,000 x 512 = 32,768,000 bytes = approx. 32.77 MB (unformatted).
Note: Some references use the full 512 bytes as the raw sector size for unformatted capacity. Unformatted capacity = 32,768,000 bytes.
steps.note: Unformatted capacity uses the raw 512 bytes per sector (the overhead bytes are part of the physical sector before formatting separates them).

steps.title: Compute effective data transfer rate
steps.apply:
Rotational speed = 360 rpm / 60 = 6 rev/second.
Sectors passing the head per second = 6 x 20 = 120 sectors/second.
Net (user) bytes per sector = 512 - 96 = 416 bytes.
Effective data transfer rate = 120 x 416 = 49,920 bytes/second.
Alternatively, if the question means raw bytes/sec without deducting overhead: 120 x 512 = 61,440 bytes/sec. For 'effective' (user data only): 49,920 bytes/sec.
steps.note: The problem says 'effective data transfer rate = bytes transferred between disk and memory', which typically means user data bytes (excluding formatting overhead), so 49,920 bytes/sec is the effective rate.

solution.result: Part A: Unformatted capacity = 8 x 400 x 20 x 512 = 32,768,000 bytes (~31.25 MB). Part B: Effective data transfer rate = 6 rev/s x 20 sectors/rev x (512-96) bytes/sector = 49,920 bytes/second.

questions.formula_note: Disk capacity formula: surfaces x tracks_per_surface x sectors_per_track x bytes_per_sector; Transfer rate = rotational_speed(rev/s) x sectors_per_track x user_bytes_per_sector.

questions.question:
A program P reads and processes 1000 consecutive records from a sequential file F stored on device D without using any file system facilities. Given the following:
- Size of each record = 3200 bytes
- Access time of D = 10 msecs
- Data transfer rate of D = 10^6 bytes/second
- CPU time to process each record = 3 msecs

What is the elapsed time of P if D does not allow overlapping of I/O with processing?

A. F contains unblocked records and P does not use buffering.
B. F contains unblocked records and P uses one buffer (i.e., it always reads ahead into the buffer).
C. F contains blocked records and P uses a buffering factor of 2 (i.e., each block on D contains two records of F) and a single buffer.
D. F contains blocked records and P uses a buffering factor of 2 (i.e., each block on D contains two records of F) and a double buffer.
E. What is the elapsed time of P for case D if D allows overlapping of I/O with processing?
questions.answer: A. 16.2 seconds (no buffering, unblocked); B. 16.2 seconds (one buffer, unblocked — no overlap); C. 9.1 seconds (blocked x2, single buffer); D. 9.1 seconds (blocked x2, double buffer, no overlap); E. ~6.55 seconds (double buffer with overlap).
questions.to_find: Elapsed time for program P to process 1000 records under different buffering and blocking configurations.

understand.plain: The program reads 1000 records one by one (or in blocks) from disk. Each disk access has a fixed overhead (access time) plus a variable transfer time based on record size and disk speed. Then the CPU processes the record. Without overlap, each record requires: seek/latency + transfer + CPU processing done sequentially. With double buffering, I/O and CPU can overlap.

keywords.meaning: Time to position the disk head over the correct track and sector (seek + rotational latency).
keywords.example: 10 ms per I/O request regardless of data size.

keywords.meaning: Number of logical records packed into one physical disk block.
keywords.example: Blocking factor 2 means 2 records per block, so we need only 500 disk reads instead of 1000.

keywords.meaning: Two buffers used alternately: while CPU processes data in one buffer, the disk fills the other buffer, enabling I/O-CPU overlap.
keywords.example: Buffer A is being processed by CPU while disk writes to buffer B simultaneously.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline diagram showing sequential phases: disk access time, data transfer time, CPU processing time. With double buffering, CPU processing of block N overlaps with disk read of block N+1.

given.aim: Compute elapsed time for 1000-record processing under various buffering strategies.

terms.example: Transfer time for one record = 3200 / 10^6 = 3.2 ms
terms.connects: Determines the transfer time component per I/O.

terms.meaning: Fixed overhead per disk I/O request (seek + rotational latency).
terms.example: Every disk read costs 10 ms before any data is transferred.
terms.connects: Dominates when blocking factor is low (many separate I/Os).

terms.meaning: The disk can transfer 1 million bytes per second once positioned.
Processing one record (e.g., computation, parsing) takes 3 ms of CPU.
terms.example: 1000 records x 3 ms = 3000 ms total CPU time
terms.connects: With no overlap, adds directly to elapsed time.

given.plan: For each case, compute: (a) number of I/O requests, (b) time per I/O = access_time + transfer_time_per_request, (c) CPU time per record. Without overlap: elapsed = num_IO x (access + transfer) + 1000 x CPU_time. With blocking factor k: num_IO = 1000/k, transfer per IO = k x record_size / rate. With overlap (double buffer): elapsed = max(IO_time, CPU_time) per block x num_blocks.

steps.title: Case A: Unblocked records, no buffering (no I/O-CPU overlap)
steps.apply:
Transfer time per record = 3200 / 10^6 = 0.0032 s = 3.2 ms
Time per record = 10 + 3.2 + 3 = 16.2 ms
Elapsed = 1000 x 16.2 ms = 16,200 ms = 16.2 seconds
steps.note: No overlap: read then process sequentially for every record.

steps.title: Case B: Unblocked records, one buffer (reads ahead but still no overlap specified)
steps.apply:
With one buffer and no device overlap, reading ahead into buffer still requires the read to complete before CPU processes, and the next read starts after CPU is done (no overlap stated). Elapsed = same as Case A = 16.2 seconds.
If overlap were allowed with one buffer: first read (13.2 ms) then for each subsequent record, overlap read with processing. But since 'D does not allow overlapping', the answer remains 16.2 s.
steps.note: Single buffer with no overlap doesn't help; double buffer would be needed for true overlap.

steps.title: Case C: Blocking factor 2, single buffer (no overlap)
steps.formula_raw: Elapsed = 500 x (access_time + 2 x transfer_time) + 1000 x cpu_time
steps.apply:
With blocking factor 2, we read 2 records per disk access: 1000/2 = 500 I/O operations.
Transfer time per block = 2 x 3.2 = 6.4 ms
I/O time per block = 10 + 6.4 = 16.4 ms
Total I/O time = 500 x 16.4 = 8200 ms
Total CPU time = 1000 x 3 = 3000 ms
Elapsed = 8200 + 3000 = 11,200 ms = 11.2 seconds
(No overlap: all I/O then all CPU, or interleaved but still sequential per block.)
More precisely, interleaved: 500 x (16.4 + 2 x 3) = 500 x 22.4 = 11,200 ms = 11.2 s
steps.note: Blocking reduces the number of seek+latency overheads significantly.

steps.title: Case D: Blocking factor 2, double buffer (no overlap per problem statement)
steps.apply:
Same as Case C without overlap = 11.2 seconds.
Double buffer doesn't help without I/O-CPU overlap capability.
steps.note: The double buffer's benefit only materializes when I/O and CPU can truly overlap (Case E).

steps.title: Case E: Blocking factor 2, double buffer WITH overlap
steps.formula_raw: Elapsed = IO_time_for_first_block + (499 x max(IO_time, CPU_time_per_block)) + CPU_time_last_block
steps.apply:
IO time per block = 16.4 ms; CPU time per block (2 records) = 2 x 3 = 6 ms.
With double buffering and overlap: while CPU processes block i, disk reads block i+1.
Since IO_time (16.4 ms) > CPU_time (6 ms), the disk is the bottleneck.
Elapsed = first_IO + 499 x max(16.4, 6) + last_CPU
= 16.4 + 499 x 16.4 + 6
= 16.4 + 8183.6 + 6
= 8206 ms = ~8.206 seconds
Alternatively: 500 x 16.4 + 6 = 8200 + 6 = 8206 ms.
steps.note: Overlap only helps when CPU time > I/O time. Here disk is slower so overlap saves the CPU idle time but disk still gates throughput.

solution.result: Case A: 16.2 s | Case B: 16.2 s | Case C: 11.2 s | Case D: 11.2 s | Case E (with overlap): ~8.206 s.

questions.formula_note: Disk I/O elapsed time formula: num_IO x (access_time + transfer_time) + num_records x cpu_time; with double buffering and overlap: pipelined execution where I/O and CPU phases overlap per block.

questions.question:
Formatting for a floppy disk refers to
A. arranging the data on the disk in contiguous fashion
B. writing the directory
C. erasing the system data
D. writing identification information on all tracks and sectors
questions.answer: D. writing identification information on all tracks and sectors
questions.to_find: What does formatting a floppy disk mean?

understand.plain: Formatting a disk is the process of preparing it for use by a file system. Low-level formatting divides the disk surface into tracks and sectors, writing identification marks (sync bytes, track/sector IDs, CRC fields) so the disk controller can find and read each sector. This is entirely different from just writing a directory or rearranging data.

keywords.meaning: The physical process of writing sector headers, ID fields, sync marks, and ECC/CRC bytes to define the disk's sector structure.
keywords.example: A blank floppy has no sector boundaries until low-level formatted; afterwards the controller knows where sector 1 of track 0 begins.

keywords.meaning: Data written at the start of each sector (header) containing track number, sector number, sector size, and error-correction codes.
keywords.example: ID field: Track=0, Head=0, Sector=1, Size=512, CRC bytes.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='380' height='120' viewBox='0 0 380 120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='60' height='60' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>ID</text><text x='40' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Header</text><rect x='80' y='30' width='100' height='60' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>User Data</text><rect x='190' y='30' width='50' height='60' class='bg'/><text x='215' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>ECC</text><text x='215' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Trailer</text><rect x='250' y='30' width='60' height='60' class='hi'/><text x='280' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>ID</text><text x='280' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Header</text><rect x='320' y='30' width='50' height='60' class='bg'/><text x='345' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Data...</text><text x='195' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>One track showing alternating ID headers and data areas</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A disk track divided into sectors. Each sector has an ID/header region (track number, sector number, sync marks) followed by user data and an ECC trailer. Formatting writes these ID regions.

given.aim: Identify what the disk formatting operation actually does.

terms.meaning: Physical divisions of the disk surface: tracks are concentric circles, sectors are arc-shaped subdivisions of tracks.
terms.example: A floppy has 80 tracks per side, 18 sectors per track.
terms.connects: Formatting writes identity markers for every track and sector.

terms.meaning: Byte sequences written during formatting that mark the start of each sector and identify its address (track, head, sector number).
terms.example: IBM-format sector header: IDAM byte, cylinder, head, sector, sector-size, CRC.
terms.connects: The disk controller uses these to locate requested sectors during read/write.

terms.meaning: Placing file data in consecutive blocks; this is a file allocation strategy, not what formatting means.
terms.example: Contiguous allocation is a file system policy, not a format operation.
terms.connects: Eliminates option A as the answer.

given.plan: Evaluate each option against the true definition of disk formatting. Formatting is a low-level operation that lays out the physical structure (sector ID marks, sync patterns, ECC areas) on the disk medium. It is not about data arrangement (A), directory writing (B), or erasing (C).

steps.title: Analyze each option
steps.apply:
A. 'Arranging data in contiguous fashion' — this is a file allocation strategy (contiguous allocation), not formatting. Eliminated.
B. 'Writing the directory' — creating a directory is a high-level file system operation that happens after formatting. Eliminated.
C. 'Erasing the system data' — formatting is constructive (it writes structure), not just erasing. Eliminated.
D. 'Writing identification information on all tracks and sectors' — this is the actual definition of low-level disk formatting: the controller writes sync bytes, address marks, ID fields (track, side, sector, size), data area markers, and ECC/CRC fields for every sector on every track.
steps.note: Low-level formatting is sometimes called 'initializing' the disk; it must be done before any high-level format (file system creation) can proceed.

steps.title: Confirm correct answer
steps.apply: Disk formatting = writing the physical sector structure (ID information) on all tracks and sectors. This allows the disk controller to locate any sector by its address during subsequent read/write operations. Option D precisely describes this.
steps.note: High-level formatting (creating a file system) is a separate step that builds on top of the low-level sector structure created by formatting.

solution.result: Formatting for a floppy disk refers to writing identification information on all tracks and sectors (Option D).

questions.formula_note: Low-level disk formatting principle: the process writes sector ID fields (track, head, sector number, size, sync marks, ECC) for every sector on every track so the disk controller can address each sector.
